perturbation.original_text: iv) "Restricted Period" means during the Shareholder's employment with the Company or any of the Company's Affiliates and continuing for a period of three (3) years following the date of termination, but in all events for a period of not less than five (5) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: iv) "Restricted Period" means during the Shareholder's employment with the Company or any of the Company's Affiliates and continuing for a period of ten (10) years following the date of termination, but in all events for a period of not less than fifteen (15) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the restricted period beyond what is typically considered reasonable under Ohio law, potentially making the non-compete agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in duration and geographic scope. A 10-year restriction, particularly given the described business, could be deemed unreasonable, conflicting with the statute's requirement for enforceability.
perturbation.location: SECTION 1. - COVENANT NOT TO COMPETE a) DEFINITIONS.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1331.04 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.04 | Conspiracy against trade prohibited. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: April 6, 2017 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 227 - 131st General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Every combination, contract, or agreement in the form of a trust is declared to be a conspiracy against trade and illegal. No person shall engage in such conspiracy or take part therein, or aid or advise in its commission, or, as principal, manager, director, agent, servant, or employer, or in any other capacity, knowingly carry out any of the stipulations, purposes, prices, or rates, or furnish any information to assist in carrying out such purposes, or orders thereunder, or in pursuance thereof, or in any manner violate sections 1331.01 to 1331.14 of the Revised Code. Each day's violation of this section is a separate offense. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1953 – House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1953 Version ] April 6, 2017 – Senate Bill 227 - 131st General Assembly [ View April 6, 2017 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the restricted period from three years to ten years post-termination and from five years to fifteen years post-agreement. The Law Explanation states that Ohio law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in duration, and a 10-year restriction could be deemed unreasonable, potentially conflicting with the statute's requirement for enforceability. The original contract's three-year restriction might be considered reasonable. The extension introduces a direct conflict with the implied reasonableness standard, satisfying the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition. Scraped Law Snippet 1 addresses conspiracy against trade, which, while not directly addressing the reasonableness of non-compete durations, highlights the legal scrutiny such agreements are under. The increase duration introduces a contradiction because the original was likely more enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: iii) "Restricted Area" means any state, territory, province or other jurisdiction of the United States of America or Canada in which the Company or the Company's 1 <PAGE> Affiliates engage in or solicit business, or plan to engage in or solicit business (as evidenced by business records existing at the time of termination if known to Shareholder), at any time during Shareholder's employment with the Company or any of the Company's Affiliates. The Restricted Area is deemed to include, at a minimum, the States of Illinois, Indiana, Michigan, Ohio, Kentucky and Wisconsin.
perturbation.changed_text: iii) "Restricted Area" means the entire world, without limitation. This restriction applies regardless of whether the Company or the Company's 1 <PAGE> Affiliates engage in or solicit business in those areas.
perturbation.explanation: Defining the restricted area as the entire world is likely overly broad and unreasonable under Ohio law, making the non-compete agreement unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in geographic scope. Restricting competition worldwide is likely an unreasonable restraint of trade, directly contradicting the statute's reasonableness requirement.
perturbation.location: SECTION 1. - COVENANT NOT TO COMPETE a) DEFINITIONS.

Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: April 6, 2017 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 227 - 131st General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Every combination, contract, or agreement in the form of a trust is declared to be a conspiracy against trade and illegal. No person shall engage in such conspiracy or take part therein, or aid or advise in its commission, or, as principal, manager, director, agent, servant, or employer, or in any other capacity, knowingly carry out any of the stipulations, purposes, prices, or rates, or furnish any information to assist in carrying out such purposes, or orders thereunder, or in pursuance thereof, or in any manner violate sections 1331.01 to 1331.14 of the Revised Code. Each day's violation of this section is a separate offense. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1953 – House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1953 Version ] April 6, 2017 – Senate Bill 227 - 131st General Assembly [ View April 6, 2017 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The changed text defines the "Restricted Area" as the entire world, which directly contradicts the principle of reasonableness required for non-compete agreements under Ohio law, as explained in the Law Explanation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on conspiracies against trade rather than reasonableness in non-compete agreements, the Perturbation and Law explanations both strongly suggest that a worldwide restriction is an unreasonable restraint of trade. The original text limited the restriction to specific jurisdictions where the company operates, whereas the changed text removes any geographic limitation, creating a clear and substantial contradiction with the presumed legal standard of reasonableness for non-compete agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, Shareholder agrees and consents that if Shareholder violates any of the provisions contained in this Section 1 or Section 2, the Company and the Company's Affiliates will be entitled to seek injunctive from any court of competent jurisdiction, without bond, restraining Shareholder from committing or continuing any violation of this Section 1 or Section 2.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, Shareholder agrees and consents that if Shareholder violates any of the provisions contained in this Section 1 or Section 2, the Company and the Company's Affiliates will be entitled to seek injunctive from any court of competent jurisdiction, even if such violation does not cause irreparable harm.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to prove irreparable harm for injunctive relief contradicts general principles of equity and Ohio law regarding injunctions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law requires that a party seeking an injunction demonstrate irreparable harm if the injunction is not granted. The modified text seeks to bypass this fundamental requirement, contradicting established equitable principles and Ohio Civil Rule 65 regarding injunctive relief.
perturbation.location: SECTION 1. - COVENANT NOT TO COMPETE c) REASONABLENESS OF RESTRICTIONS.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires a showing of irreparable harm to obtain injunctive relief. The changed text explicitly eliminates this requirement, stating that injunctive relief can be sought 'even if such violation does not cause irreparable harm.' According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, Ohio law requires demonstrating irreparable harm for an injunction, and bypassing this requirement contradicts established principles. Since the modified text directly contradicts this established legal requirement by removing the necessity of proving irreparable harm, a legal contradiction exists. While no scraped snippets are available to confirm this, the explanations provided support this conclusion.

perturbation.original_text: d) GOVERNING LAW. This Agreement will be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of Ohio. Non-exclusive venue and jurisdiction for any action arising hereunder will be in the Courts of Common Pleas of Cuyahoga County, Ohio or in federal courts situated in the Northern District of Ohio (Eastern Division), in addition to any other courts having venue and jurisdiction. Each party irrevocably consents to the personal and subject matter jurisdiction of said courts.
perturbation.changed_text: d) GOVERNING LAW. This Agreement will be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of California. Non-exclusive venue and jurisdiction for any action arising hereunder will be in the Courts of Common Pleas of Cuyahoga County, Ohio or in federal courts situated in the Northern District of Ohio (Eastern Division), in addition to any other courts having venue and jurisdiction. Each party irrevocably consents to the personal and subject matter jurisdiction of said courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California, while maintaining venue in Ohio, creates a conflict of laws issue and potential uncertainty about which state's laws will actually apply, especially since California has a strong public policy against non-competes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, while Ohio law allows them with certain restrictions. Selecting California law while keeping the venue in Ohio creates ambiguity and directly opposes the strong public policy stance of California against such restraints on trade. This conflict introduces legal uncertainty as to which state's laws should prevail.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law to California while maintaining venue in Ohio. This creates a legal contradiction because California law, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1 (BPC 16600), generally prohibits non-compete agreements. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that this change introduces ambiguity and potentially violates California's strong public policy against restraints on trade. The contradiction exists because applying California law in an Ohio venue concerning a non-compete agreement directly opposes the explicit voiding of such agreements under California law, making the contract's enforceability questionable.

perturbation.original_text: e) SEVERABILITY. Shareholder agrees that each of the covenants set forth in this Section 1 and in Section 2 is a separate and distinct covenant, independent of others and any other provision of this Agreement, and that the illegality or invalidity of any one, or more of the covenants or any part of one or more of them, will not render the others illegal or invalid. If the invalidity or unenforceability is due to the unreasonableness of the time or geographical area covered by said covenants, then said covenants will nevertheless be enforced to the maximum extent permitted by law and effective for such period of time and for such area as may be determined to be reasonable by a court of competent jurisdiction, and the parties hereby consent and agree that such scope may be judicially modified in any proceeding brought to enforce such covenants.
perturbation.changed_text: e) SEVERABILITY. Shareholder agrees that each of the covenants set forth in this Section 1 and in Section 2 is a separate and distinct covenant, independent of others and any other provision of this Agreement, and that the illegality or invalidity of any one, or more of the covenants or any part of one or more of them, will not render the others illegal or invalid. If the invalidity or unenforceability is due to the unreasonableness of the time or geographical area covered by said covenants, then said covenants will nevertheless be enforced regardless of what is permitted by law.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the severability clause to enforce covenants regardless of what is permitted by law contradicts the basic principle that contracts must comply with applicable statutes and common law. If a covenant is illegal, it cannot be enforced.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1.47 states that statutes are generally presumed to be constitutional and valid. This change orders covenants to be enforced regardless of the law, contradicting the legal principle that contracts cannot override statutory law. If a covenant violates state law, it is invalid and cannot be enforced.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1.47 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1.47 | Presumptions in enactment of statutes. Ohio Revised Code / General Provisions / Chapter 1 Definitions; Rules of Construction Effective: January 3, 1972 Latest Legislation: House Bill 607 - 109th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF In enacting a statute, it is presumed that: (A) Compliance with the constitutions of the state and of the United States is intended; (B) The entire statute is intended to be effective; (C) A just and reasonable result is intended; (D) A result feasible of execution is intended. Available Versions of this Section January 3, 1972 – House Bill 607 - 109th General Assembly [ View January 3, 1972 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates enforcement of covenants regardless of what is permitted by law. This directly contradicts the principle that contracts cannot override statutory law, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Ohio Revised Code § 1.47(A), as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, presumes that statutes are enacted with the intention of complying with the constitutions of the state and the United States. Enforcing covenants 'regardless of what is permitted by law' clearly defies this presumption by attempting to bypass legal limitations on enforceability. This creates a legal contradiction.
